title: Tile Installation Business
metaTitle: How to Start a Tile Installation Business: Real Costs, Earnings, and Steps
metaDescription: Honest breakdown of starting a tile installation business: real startup costs, earnings by experience, contractor licensing, waterproofing, and what most people get wrong.
bestFor: People who want a skilled, physical craft with high earning potential and don't mind precise, demanding work
biggestRisk: A failed shower waterproofing or cracked-tile job that leads to expensive callbacks, leaks, and lost reputation
whatItIs: A tile installation business installs ceramic, porcelain, stone, and glass tile on floors, walls, showers, backsplashes, and countertops for homeowners, remodelers, and general contractors. It is a true skilled craft: success depends on substrate preparation, dead-level layout, waterproofing wet areas correctly, and clean cuts and grout lines. Tile is one of the trades where the difference between an amateur and a pro is immediately visible and where mistakes, especially in showers, surface as expensive leaks and failures months later. The work is physically demanding and detail-obsessed, which is exactly why skilled installers can command strong rates.
dailyReality: A typical day starts with loading materials and tools, then setting up on a job site that may be a fresh remodel or an occupied home. You spend hours on your knees and feet, prepping substrate, applying waterproofing or backer board, laying out the pattern, mixing thinset, setting and leveling tile, cutting around fixtures with a wet saw, and grouting. The work is exacting; a layout decision in the first hour determines whether the whole room looks professional. Between jobs you measure and quote, order materials, coordinate with general contractors and homeowners, and handle the wear on your body that comes with kneeling and lifting all day.

startupCostsItemized.item: Wet saw (tile saw) and tile cutter
Hand tools (trowels, floats, levels, spacers, mixing paddle)
Knee pads, dust control, safety gear
Work van or truck setup (if not owned)
Business registration / LLC and contractor license (where required)
Initial material float for first jobs
Website, Google Business Profile, photos, marketing

realEarnings.yearOne: Skilled solo installers starting their own business typically net $3,000 to $6,000 per month in year one, depending on how quickly they line up remodelers and homeowner work. Those still building speed and a reputation earn at the lower end while they prove their finish quality.
realEarnings.experienced: Experienced installers with two or more years of self-employment and a steady referral base commonly report $6,000 to $14,000 per month. Tile labor often runs $7 to $20+ per square foot installed depending on tile size, pattern, and region, and complex showers and large-format tile command premium rates.
realEarnings.topEarners: Top installers and small crews specializing in high-end showers, large-format porcelain, and custom stone gross $200,000 to $400,000+ per year, and operators who run multiple crews go higher. Reaching that takes years of refined skill, a reputation among designers and builders, and a willingness to manage labor instead of always being on the tools.
realEarnings.whatAffectsMost: Finish quality and specialization drive earnings most. An installer trusted with curbless showers, large-format porcelain, and intricate patterns charges far more than a generalist. Reliability with general contractors and designers turns one job into a steady pipeline.
realEarnings.perHour: Effective rates for skilled solo installers commonly run $50 to $120 per hour of actual work, before measuring, quoting, material runs, and unpaid prep. Counting all the unbilled time, realistic blended rates are often $45 to $90 per hour.

howToStart: Before launch: Get real skill first. Tile is unforgiving, so apprentice, work for an established installer, or complete hands-on training (NTCA workshops, manufacturer certifications). Master substrate prep, layout, and especially shower waterproofing before charging customers.

Week 1: Check your state's contractor licensing rules and the dollar threshold at which tile work needs a license, register the business, and get general liability insurance. Many states require a license once a job exceeds a set value, so know your limits before bidding.

Weeks 2-3: Assemble your core tools, build a simple portfolio from your best practice and prior work, and create a Google Business Profile. Reach out to local remodelers, general contractors, kitchen-and-bath showrooms, and designers who feed steady tile work.
Weeks 3-6: Take your first jobs at fair (not cheapest) prices, photograph every result, and ask for reviews. Track your real time and material costs per square foot so you price profitably.
Months 2-12: Build a referral pipeline with contractors and designers, specialize in higher-value work like showers and large-format tile, and decide whether to add a helper as demand grows.

mustHave: Genuine tile-setting skill, substrate prep, layout, cutting, and clean grout work
Correct shower and wet-area waterproofing knowledge (this is where failures happen)
Physical durability for kneeling, lifting, and detailed work all day
canLearn: Estimating and bidding by the square foot for profit
Working with large-format porcelain, mosaics, and natural stone
Coordinating with general contractors and managing a job schedule
separators: Mastering complex showers, curbless designs, and large-format tile that few installers do well
A flawless, repeatable finish that designers and builders trust enough to refer
Reliability and clean job sites that keep contractors coming back with steady work

whatPeopleGetWrong: Botching shower waterproofing, which leaks months later and leads to costly tear-outs and ruined reputation
Skipping or rushing substrate prep, so tiles crack, lippage shows, and floors fail
Underbidding by the square foot without accounting for prep, cuts, and pattern complexity
Starting with too little real skill and learning expensive lessons on paying customers' homes
Ignoring the tile-work licensing threshold and getting fined or unable to pull permits on larger remodels and additions
Neglecting their body, no knee protection or pacing, and burning out physically within a few years

toolsAndEquipment.note: Essential for clean cuts; a quality saw pays off on large-format and stone.

toolsAndEquipment.item: Trowels, floats, levels, and leveling clips
toolsAndEquipment.note: Core setting tools; leveling systems reduce lippage on large tile.
For consistent thinset and grout; consistency affects bond strength.
Membranes and substrate for wet areas; never cut corners here.

toolsAndEquipment.item: Knee pads, dust extraction, and safety gear
toolsAndEquipment.note: Protects your body and lungs over a long career.

toolsAndEquipment.item: Work van or truck and racking
toolsAndEquipment.note: For hauling tile, tools, and saw between sites.

methods: Relationships with general contractors, remodelers, and kitchen-and-bath showrooms who subcontract tile work
Referrals from interior designers and builders who need a reliable, high-quality installer
A Google Business Profile and portfolio site with sharp photos of finished showers and floors
Local Facebook groups, Nextdoor, and home-service platforms for direct homeowner jobs
Word of mouth and reviews from completed jobs, which compound quickly for quality work
howToFindCustomers.whereCustomersAre: Homeowners doing kitchen and bathroom remodels, general contractors and remodelers needing a dependable tile sub, and designers specifying tile on projects. The highest-value, most consistent work comes through contractor and designer relationships.
howToFindCustomers.timeToClientBase: Skilled installers often land first jobs within two to six weeks, but a steady pipeline through contractors and referrals usually takes six to twelve months of proving finish quality and reliability.
howToFindCustomers.wasteOfTime: Cheap printed ads and competing on lowest price. Tile is bought on trust and visible quality, so a strong photo portfolio and contractor relationships convert far better than discount marketing.

howItScales.toFullTime: Yes. A skilled solo installer can reach a strong full-time income within the first year or two by lining up remodelers and homeowner work and pricing for quality. The solo ceiling is set by your hours and your body.
howItScales.hireAndStepBack: Possible but demanding. You can hire and train helpers and run multiple crews, but tile quality is hard to delegate, your reputation rides on every job. Stepping back requires trusted lead setters and tight control over layout, waterproofing, and grout finish on every job.
howItScales.sellable: Moderately sellable. A business with crews, contractor relationships, systems, and a brand can sell, but a pure solo operation is largely tied to the founder's hands. Documented processes and recurring contractor accounts increase value.
howItScales.whatScalingRequires: Training reliable installers without losing finish quality, systems for estimating and scheduling, contractor and designer relationships that feed steady work, and a shift from being on the tools to managing crews and bids.

strongFit: You already have or are committed to building real tile-setting skill
You take pride in precise, visible craftsmanship and detail
You are physically durable and can handle demanding, kneeling work daily
You can build dependable relationships with contractors and designers
poorFit: You want low-skill, low-physical, or hands-off work
You are impatient with prep, layout, and the slow path to mastery
You cut corners or rush, waterproofing and prep punish that
You won't learn your state's tile-licensing thresholds or carry contractor liability insurance
askYourself: Is my finish quality genuinely good enough that contractors and homeowners would refer me?
Do I understand shower waterproofing well enough to avoid leaks that destroy reputations?
Can my body handle years of kneeling and lifting, and am I pricing to make it worthwhile?

faqs.question: Do I need a contractor license to install tile?
faqs.answer: It depends on your state and the size of the job. Many states require a contractor's license once a project exceeds a dollar threshold (often a few hundred to a few thousand dollars in labor and materials), while small jobs may be exempt. Some states regulate tile under a general or specialty contractor license. Confirm your state's tile-work threshold before you quote a shower or floor job, because setting tile unlicensed above that dollar limit risks fines and unenforceable contracts.

faqs.question: How much can a tile installer earn?
faqs.answer: Skilled solo installers commonly net $6,000 to $14,000 per month once established, with tile labor running roughly $7 to $20+ per square foot depending on tile size, pattern, and region. Complex showers and large-format work pay the most. Top installers and small crews specializing in high-end work earn well into six figures annually.

faqs.question: Why is waterproofing such a big deal?
faqs.answer: Showers and wet areas are where tile jobs most often fail. If waterproofing behind the tile is done wrong, water gets into the wall and subfloor and causes mold, rot, and expensive tear-outs months or years later. Mastering modern waterproofing systems is non-negotiable before you take on bathroom and shower work.

faqs.question: Can I learn tile on the job with no experience?
faqs.answer: You can start learning, but you should not run a tile business with no real skill. Tile is unforgiving and mistakes are visible and costly. Apprentice with an experienced installer, take NTCA workshops or manufacturer training, and practice extensively before charging customers, because early mistakes on real homes are expensive to fix.

faqs.question: How do I price tile jobs?
faqs.answer: Most installers price by the square foot for labor, adjusting for tile size, pattern complexity, substrate prep, and demolition. Large-format tile, mosaics, intricate patterns, and showers command higher rates. The key is to account for prep, cuts, and waste, then track your real time per job so your effective rate stays profitable rather than just winning bids.

faqs.question: Is tile work hard on your body?
faqs.answer: Yes. It involves constant kneeling, lifting heavy tile and materials, and detailed work in awkward positions. Quality knee protection, dust control, and pacing matter for a long career. Many installers manage the wear by specializing, hiring helpers for demolition and hauling, and being selective about the most physically punishing jobs.

faqs.question: Where does the steady work come from?
faqs.answer: The most consistent, higher-value work flows through general contractors, remodelers, kitchen-and-bath showrooms, and interior designers who need a reliable tile sub. Homeowner jobs from Google and neighborhood groups fill gaps, but contractor and designer relationships are what turn occasional jobs into a steady pipeline.

dataSources: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Tile and Stone Setters occupational employment and wage data
National Tile Contractors Association (NTCA), installation standards and training resources
Tile Council of North America (TCNA), installation and waterproofing standards
Home-service cost guides (Angi, HomeAdvisor) for reported per-square-foot tile pricing
State contractor licensing boards for licensing thresholds and requirements
